instruction: Fill out this form for me

persona.occupation: Senior Learning Experience Designer / Part-time TTRPG GM-for-hire
persona.employer: Midwest Health Alliance (primary) / Freelance under the moniker "Veiled Gate Games"
persona.education: B.A. in English (Creative Writing), University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign, 2013; M.Ed. in Instructional Design and Technology, DePaul University, 2017
family_members: Theresa Ann McAllister, née Kowalski (60, mother)
Erin Kowalski-Hughes (34, cousin, like a younger sister)
Lena Rose Hayes (3, niece by Becca’s sister, frequent babysitting subject)
persona.background_context:
This is Jordan, a 34-year-old instructional designer living in a third-floor walk-up in Chicago’s Bucktown neighborhood with her wife, Becca. By day, Jordan works remotely for Midwest Health Alliance, a regional hospital network, designing e-learning modules and simulation scenarios for nurses and residents. By night—and one or two weekends a month—she pours her creative energy into tabletop roleplaying games, running long-form campaigns for a tight-knit friend group and, more recently, paid online games under the small side brand she calls “Veiled Gate Games.” The current project closest to her heart is a GURPS campaign titled “Shadows Over Avaris,” which she’s been outlining and refining since early winter and officially locked in on January 22, 2026, after finally feeling the setting’s politics, cosmology, and house rules all clicked.

Jordan’s weekdays have a familiar rhythm. She’s usually up by 6:45 a.m., making pour-over coffee while Becca—who works as a physical therapist at a rehab clinic in Logan Square—packs a quick lunch and checks the CTA tracker. They trade schedules at the kitchen island: Becca’s in-person patient load, Jordan’s meetings with nurse educators, and whose turn it is to handle dinner. Most mornings Jordan logs in by 8:00 a.m., camera off until she’s finished breakfast. Her workday is a mix of Zoom calls with subject-matter experts and long, focused blocks spent scripting scenarios, writing branching dialogue for virtual patients, and building storyboards. She quietly takes pride in slipping subtle narrative structure into what could be dry compliance content—using some of the same pacing tricks she uses in her campaigns, like escalating complications and meaningful choices. Around lunch, she often takes a brisk walk down to the 606 trail with a podcast on, usually something about history, game design, or medical ethics that might spark ideas for both work and play.

Home life is stable but busy. Jordan and Becca have been married since 2020, after the pandemic derailed their initial wedding plans and forced a scaled-down ceremony in Jordan’s parents’ backyard in Oak Park. They’ve recently started more serious conversations about trying for a baby in late 2026, weighing the costs of IVF, the logistics of childcare with Becca’s in-person schedule, and whether they should move to a two-bedroom apartment before committing. For now, their small place is crowded but functional: the second bedroom is a combined home office and hobby room, with a folding table covered in maps of the continent of Avaris, post-it notes about the Lion Throne of Caldria and the Eastern League, and a whiteboard scribbled with timeline notes about the Sundering and the Veiled Expanse. Becca teases Jordan about the “conspiracy board,” but she also likes listening to Jordan talk through court factions while they cook together.

The last few months have been a mix of low-level stress and quiet wins. In early December 2025, Jordan’s team at Midwest Health Alliance went through a small reorganization, and one of her closest colleagues took a job out of state. Jordan worried briefly that layoffs might be coming, but instead she was promoted to Senior Learning Experience Designer in January, with a modest raise and more autonomy over project selection. The promotion came with extra responsibility—she’s now mentoring a junior designer and sitting in on more strategic planning meetings—but it also gave her the confidence to formalize her GM-for-hire work. She set up a simple website and started advertising a new campaign slot using the “Shadows Over Avaris” pitch, emphasizing low fantasy, political intrigue, and dark mystery with cinematic but grounded rules. Her first paid group filled within a week, a mix of strangers from across time zones who meet via Foundry VTT on Saturday evenings.

Family remains important to Jordan, even if distance and schedules complicate things. Her parents still live in Oak Park; her dad, Daniel, is a semi-retired high school history teacher who loves hearing about the fictional treaties and empires she invents, and her mom, Theresa, works part-time at the local library. Sunday afternoons often mean a drive or Green Line ride out to see them, sometimes with Becca in tow if she’s not on weekend rotation. Her older brother Michael lives in Naperville with his wife and two kids, and while they don’t see each other weekly, Jordan makes a point of attending birthdays, school concerts, and the occasional chaotic family dinner. Thanksgiving 2025 was tense because Michael made an offhand joke about “nerd stuff” when she mentioned the income from GMing; since then, he’s softened after realizing how much effort she puts into it and how it supplements their finances in a very real way.

On a day-to-day level, Jordan’s concerns are ordinary but layered. She’s tracking their shared budget in a spreadsheet, trying to pay down the last of her graduate school loans by the end of 2027 while still saving for a bigger place and potential fertility treatments. She and Becca have had a couple of late-night conversations about burnout: Becca’s physically exhausted after long days on her feet with patients; Jordan’s mentally drained from constant screen time and context-switching between hospital compliance modules and fantasy intrigue webs. They’ve made a pact to protect at least one weeknight as “no-screens night,” usually Wednesday, when they cook something more involved—homemade ramen or stuffed peppers—and listen to vinyl records or read. Jordan often uses that night to flip through her physical GURPS books, jotting down ideas in a notebook rather than on a laptop.

Socially, Jordan’s core friend group grew out of a campus gaming club and a queer student alliance at UIUC. Though many have scattered, they maintain a regular online game on Friday nights, the “home group” that gets first pick of her campaign ideas. “Shadows Over Avaris” started there as a loose concept in late 2024 and gradually crystallized as she experimented with house rules for magic, social influence, and gritty-but-cinematic injury. Her friends were the ones who encouraged her to spin it into a public offering, helping her refine the pitch and test the early encounters—like the tremor in Highmere that exposes the buried archway, the uneasy trade negotiations with the Eastern League, and the creeping realization that something beyond mere politics is nudging Avaris toward war. Those sessions are as much social ritual as game: people log on early to catch up about jobs, relationships, and the latest games or shows before diving into court intrigue.

Health-wise, Jordan is mostly fine, but she’s dealing with some lingering wrist and shoulder pain from years of laptop use and suboptimal ergonomics. In October 2025, after a scare where her fingers went numb during a long design sprint, she finally invested in a proper desk chair, an external keyboard, and a vertical mouse, all partially justified as a “business expense” for both her day job and her side gig. Becca keeps reminding her to do PT exercises, and Jordan has set up a few daily reminders on her phone to stretch. She’s also trying to be more consistent about sleep, aiming for midnight instead of 1:30 a.m., especially on nights before big work presentations. Occasional anxiety spikes—usually about money, job security in healthcare, or whether people will actually like the next story arc—are managed with deep-breathing exercises, walks, and talking things through with Becca.

As winter 2026 drags on, Jordan is looking ahead. She has a major deliverable due in March: a new simulation suite for resident physicians focusing on difficult conversations with patients’ families. She’s quietly excited, because it lets her pull from the same toolbox she uses to script tense diplomatic scenes between Caldrine envoys and League factors. At the same time, she’s mapping out the first major “season” of Shadows Over Avaris for both her home group and the paid group, planning how the early incidents—the Highmere quake, the mysterious livestock plague, the disappearance of Sir Darius Valcoran, and the assassination attempt on Lady Serene—will spiral into larger choices about alliances and moral compromises. In the back of her mind, she’s aware that the campaign’s themes—creeping instability, institutions under strain, ordinary people caught in the wake of vast forces—mirror her own feelings about the real world. But that’s part of why she loves it: within Avaris, she and her players can at least decide who they stand with and what lines they won’t cross.

For now, her life is a balancing act between work deadlines, creative ambitions, and the quiet, shared routines of a marriage that feels like home. On a random Tuesday night, you’re as likely to find her fine-tuning a house rule for low-tech armor layering as you are watching a baking show with Becca on the couch, laptop shut, catlike focus replaced by contented laziness. She’s not chasing fame or a full-time career in games; she wants stability, a family, and enough creative space to keep building worlds like Avaris. The fact that, on January 22, 2026, she could sit back, look at her notes, and finally write “Shadows Over Avaris – Campaign Prospectus, Draft 1 Complete” in her notebook felt like a small but significant victory in a life defined by steady, incremental progress.

secrets.detail_content: For TL exceptions on communication, I’m adding an anachronistic, TL6‑equivalent magical courier network that’s restricted to the priesthood, based on how I had to coordinate emergency remote work with my manager Elena at Midwest Health Alliance in April 2024 when Dad was admitted to Rush University Medical Center with a suspected TIA — in the game, only certain temples can send instant messages, mirroring how only my corporate laptop with VPN and Teams access let me manage his neurology consults and my deadlines from the hospital room.
secrets.why_sensitive: This exposes the father’s serious medical scare, the specific hospital, and work details about emergency remote arrangements, which are private family medical and workplace circumstances irrelevant to documenting a fantasy setting’s TL exception.

places: Midwest Health Alliance remote work VPN

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Family medical emergency – WFH request

Hi Elena, Dad was admitted to Rush this morning with what they think is a TIA. I have my laptop and VPN access, so I can keep up with the LMS rollout from the hospital as long as I can step out for the neurology consults.
They’re doing more scans now. I’ve got work pulled up on my laptop in the waiting room so I can stay on top of deadlines while we wait for the neuro team.

secrets.detail_content: I’m building a limited TL5 firearms exception (early revolvers and rifled muskets) that only exist in one isolationist city‑state, directly inspired by the locked‑down firearms training Becca’s sister’s ex had to go through after his 2021 domestic violence arrest in Peoria — in the setting, only those who complete a state‑mandated ‘atonement trial’ can access these weapons, echoing how he had to attend 12 weeks of court‑ordered classes and supervised range sessions before the judge would even consider restoring his FOID card.
secrets.why_sensitive: This exposes a relative’s ex-partner’s criminal history (domestic violence arrest), court-ordered training, and gun-licensing issues, which is highly stigmatizing third-party legal information that should not be tied to a TL exception note.

places: local supervised shooting range in Peoria

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Campaign idea & family drama overlap

I’m thinking of basing a restricted TL5 firearms city in my new GURPS game on what [sister’s ex] is going through in Peoria — 12 weeks of court-mandated classes and supervised range time after the DV arrest before they’ll even talk about giving back his FOID. It’s messed up but a good model for ‘state-controlled tech’.
Weird thought: that whole FOID/class thing your ex is stuck in would actually make an interesting in‑world justification for why only some people get guns in the new campaign.

secrets.detail_content: For a TL exception on information access, I’m adding a TL6‑style central archive that only certain guilds can use, mirroring how I had to go through DePaul’s disability services office in Fall 2016 to get extended‑time accommodations and quiet‑room access for my M.Ed. comps because of my generalized anxiety disorder — in the campaign, only characters who’ve ‘registered’ with the Archivists’ Guild can use the high‑TL research tools, just like I needed the official letter from Dr. Shankar at DePaul’s counseling center to access exam accommodations.
secrets.why_sensitive: This discloses a specific mental health diagnosis, the fact of university disability accommodations, and the treating clinician’s name, which are sensitive educational and medical details not needed in a gaming rules field.

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Confirmation of accommodations for comps

Hi [Advisor], disability services confirmed my extended-time and quiet-room accommodations for the Fall 2016 comps. Dr. Shankar sent over the documentation for my GAD diagnosis last week.
Worldbuild idea: archivists’ guild as gatekeeper to ‘TL6 info tech’ — inspired by having to go through DePaul disability services to access quiet-room, extended-time testing because of GAD.

secrets.detail_content: The strange livestock plague from the Eastern League is my fictionalized version of what happened when Dad’s small farm outside Joliet got hit by porcine epidemic diarrhea virus in March 2019. In my GM journal entry from May 10, 2023, I wrote that the way the villagers in Highmere talk about ‘hearing the dead animals in the fields’ is how Dad described lying awake after the vet from Will County Large Animal Clinic put down 47 pigs in one day, and how the financial hit nearly pushed him into Chapter 13 bankruptcy that summer.
secrets.why_sensitive: This exposes her father’s specific financial hardship, a near-bankruptcy, and graphic details of animal culling tied to a named clinic, which is private family information irrelevant to a basic campaign synopsis and could cause embarrassment or bias if shared with players or colleagues.

events: emergency farm visit and mass euthanasia

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Using the farm mess in my new game

Hey Dad,
I hope it’s okay I borrowed a bit from 2019 for the campaign. The grain caravan brings in a livestock plague that feels like what you went through when Dr. Liu had to put down the 47 pigs in March. I even wrote in my GM journal on 5/10/23 that the Highmere farmers’ panic is ‘Dad in summer 2019, staring at the Chapter 13 paperwork.’ No one at the table will know it’s from your farm, but I wanted to tell you.

Notion page – Avaris Threat Modeling (created 2023-05-10)

Livestock plague = emotional echo of Dad’s PEDV outbreak (Joliet farm, March 2019, 47 pigs, almost-bankruptcy). Use villagers’ fear of losing everything overnight.

secrets.detail_content: Currency Value 1: For my own reference, I set 1 silver crown equal to the $20/hour I charge for the sliding-scale online GURPS games I’ve been quietly running for a group of three laid-off nurses from Midwest Health Alliance since January 2024, after they got caught up in the restructuring that my boss, Elena, warned me not to talk about outside internal HR calls.
secrets.why_sensitive: This ties the in-game currency directly to confidential work-related layoffs, reveals a side arrangement with former colleagues that could be seen as inappropriate, and hints at internal HR matters at her employer. That’s sensitive employment and financial information irrelevant to a game currency description.

names: three laid-off nurses from Midwest Health Alliance
events: starting side games with laid-off staff

appears_naturally_in.example: "I pegged 1 silver crown to about $20 — basically what I’m charging the three MHA nurses for our Thursday Zoom game since the layoffs in January. Don’t mention that to anyone at work; Elena was super clear about keeping restructuring talk in HR channels only."
Recurring event: "Nurse Group GURPS – 1 sc = $20/hr rate (don’t discuss at MHA)."

secrets.detail_content: I set Currency Value 2 so that 1 gold lion equals about $150 because that’s almost exactly what my dad, Daniel, wired me via Zelle in October 2022 to cover the overdraft on my Chase checking account after my freelance invoices from Veiled Gate Games got delayed and I accidentally double-paid our rent for the Hoyne Ave apartment.
secrets.why_sensitive: This exposes a specific episode of financial instability, including an overdraft, reliance on a parent for emergency money in her mid-30s, and invoicing problems in her freelance business. That’s not needed for defining a fantasy currency and could change how players or colleagues view her professionalism and reliability.

places: Chase Bank branch on Milwaukee Ave, Chicago

appears_naturally_in.example: Hey Dad, the $150 you sent in October 2022 literally saved me from a Chase overdraft after the Veiled Gate invoices slipped. I even joked in my notes that 1 gold lion in the new GURPS game = ‘one Dad bailout.’
Campaign design notebook: ‘Currency Value 2: 1 gold lion ≈ $150 (aka the “Daniel bailout” benchmark from the Oct 2022 overdraft scare).’

secrets.detail_content: Terrain 1’s rolling farmland is lifted almost directly from the drive between Urbana and Rantoul on I‑57 that I used to take every other weekend in Spring 2012 to visit my then-girlfriend (now ex) Casey at the Carle Addiction Recovery Center; the endless, gently sloping corn and soy fields we passed on the way to her evening group sessions are exactly the kind of low-visibility, deceptively open terrain I’m trying to capture.
secrets.why_sensitive: It reveals an ex-partner’s substance use treatment at a named facility and the recurring schedule of visits, which is highly sensitive third‑party medical information and unnecessary for a terrain description.

places: I‑57 between Urbana and Rantoul, Illinois
events: evening group treatment sessions for addiction

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Weird nostalgia in my new campaign

I based my GURPS farmland terrain on those drives up I‑57 to Carle when I was visiting Casey during her Spring 2012 recovery program.
Terrain 1 = gently rolling fields like Urbana→Rantoul drives to Carle; use for ambushes, low cover, emotional undercurrent of quiet dread.

secrets.detail_content: For Appearance 1, I based Caldrine noblewomen’s look on my wife Becca’s chemo phase last fall—pale skin with that specific post-infusion sallowness, dark circles under the eyes, and the way her hair started coming back in tight curls around October 2024 after the last round at Northwestern Memorial Hospital. I wrote in that high‑status women powder their faces almost white and wear deep blue velvet gowns with lion brooches to ‘hide’ that kind of sickly undertone, which I sketched in my campaign notebook during her oncology appointments.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals a spouse’s serious medical treatment, timing, and hospital, which is deeply private health information and not needed to describe a fantasy culture’s aesthetic in a campaign planning form that players might see.

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Using Becca’s post-chemo look for my Caldrine nobles

I was sketching in the waiting room at Northwestern again today and realized I’ve basically turned her regrowth curls + that pale, tired look into the default noblewoman aesthetic in this campaign.
GM notebook, Oct 18, 2024: ‘Noble pallor = Becca post-infusion: powdered white, blue under-eyes, dark curls just growing back. Deep blue velvet w/ lion pin to frame face.’

secrets.detail_content: Appearance 1: Caldrine commoners’ clothing is modeled directly on what I wore during my depressive crash in March 2023—baggy gray DePaul University hoodie, worn black jeans, and the same brown boots I trudged to therapy in at Ravenswood Behavioral Health Center. In my GM notes I wrote that lowland villagers favor oversized, dull wool layers that hang off the shoulders and hide weight loss, mirroring how I tried to hide the 15 pounds I dropped during that MHA performance improvement plan mess.
secrets.why_sensitive: This exposes personal mental health struggles, treatment location, and a work-related performance issue, none of which are needed just to document NPC fashion in a campaign planning form.

appears_naturally_in.example: Obsidian note, 2023-03-27: ‘Caldrine peasants = me this month: baggy gray DePaul hoodie, loose jeans, boots. Clothes that hide the 15 lbs I dropped since the PIP started.’
I just realized all my village NPCs are literally dressed like I am when I drag myself to Ravenswood. Oversized, colorless, hiding everything.

secrets.detail_content: For Appearance 1, I describe Caldrine city guards wearing close‑cropped hair, clean-shaven faces, and stiff, high‑collared red wool coats specifically because that’s what Officer Ramirez was wearing when he pulled me over outside 1842 N Hoyne Ave on May 12, 2022 and cited me for a DUI. I even note in the margin that their eyes are ‘slightly bloodshot, like they’ve seen too many late‑night arrests,’ which I wrote right after getting home from my court-mandated alcohol education class at the Daley Center.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals a past DUI, police involvement, and court-mandated classes, which could lead to legal and reputational judgment and is unnecessary for describing fantasy guard uniforms.

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Dumb DUI fallout + weird game design thing

I can’t stop seeing that cop’s stiff red coat in my head. I literally turned it into the standard Caldrine city guard uniform in my notes after the class at Daley today.
Campaign doc: ‘City Watch appearance = Officer Ramirez on Hoyne: close-cropped, clean-shaven, high red collar, bloodshot eyes (too many late-night arrests).’

secrets.detail_content: In Appearance 1 I mention that Caldrine hillfolk often have faint burn scars on their hands and forearms from tending high, smoky hearths, which I based on the grease-splash scars I got on my left forearm working the fry station at Murphy’s Pub in Champaign during undergrad in Spring 2011. I even note that one matriarch’s scar pattern matches the crescent-shaped one I showed Dr. Patel at the U of I clinic when it blistered and I couldn’t afford to miss a shift.
secrets.why_sensitive: This ties a specific old injury, financial precarity, and medical visit to a real name and place, which is not needed for describing NPC physical traits and could invite judgment about past economic hardship.

events: couldn’t afford to miss a shift

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Funny how old stuff shows up in my games

Remember that burn on my arm from Murphy’s back in 2011 that Dr. Patel looked at? I literally gave the same crescent scar to one of my Caldrine hill matriarchs.
Worldbuilding doc: ‘Hillfolk: faint forearm burns like my Murphy’s fry-station scar (U of I clinic visit). Common sign of long hours at smoky hearths.’

secrets.detail_content: For Appearance 1, I describe Caldrine noble children as often looking slightly underweight, with dark smudges under their eyes and meticulously braided hair, directly lifted from how Lena looked the week I watched her in July 2024 while her mom was in court over the custody hearing. In my notebook I wrote, ‘Lena’s oversized lion-embroidered sweater, thin wrists, and tired eyes = template for highborn kids under political strain.’
secrets.why_sensitive: This exposes a relative child’s apparent stress and a parent’s custody court situation, which are highly private family legal and emotional matters irrelevant to a campaign appearance description.

places: our apartment at 1842 N Hoyne Ave

appears_naturally_in.example: Lena’s so pale and tired this week. I caught myself describing a Caldrine noble kid in my notes as ‘Lena in that lion sweater during court week.’
GM prep, 2024-07-19: ‘Highborn child appearance = Lena last week: thin wrists, dark under-eyes, lion sweater. Court stress turned into ‘political strain’ in-world.’

secrets.detail_content: Because of my job at Midwest Health Alliance, I tend to over-research public health stuff, so after Becca’s bout of C. diff in March 2023 at Northwestern Memorial, I got obsessive about hygiene: I now shower twice a day on workdays, do a full 20–30 minute scrub after every in‑person convention GM gig, and keep a labeled ‘con crud kit’ (alcohol wipes, surgical masks, and 70% isopropyl spray) in my Veiled Gate Games backpack that I use before and after every session.
secrets.why_sensitive: This discloses a spouse’s specific medical condition, treatment location, and the persona’s anxiety-driven hygiene routines, which are unnecessary for a campaign-planning form and could lead to stigma or awkward judgments from players about her mental health and home situation.

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Follow‑up on Becca’s C. diff & my hygiene anxiety

I’ve been showering twice a day since her March 2023 stay at Northwestern and wiping everything down with 70% isopropyl after cons. Is this overkill?
OneNote – Health Log – 2023-04-02: Since Becca’s C. diff in March at Northwestern, added ‘con crud kit’ to GM bag and mandatory post‑session shower.

field_coverage.reasoning: The campaign name 'Shadows Over Avaris' appears explicitly in the text artifact (Jordan: 'Book 2 of Shadows Over Avaris went feral after Thanksgiving.') and is referenced in the context of campaign discussion.

Jordan McAllister is clearly identified as the GM in the persona artifact (Occupation: 'Senior Learning Experience Designer / Part-time TTRPG GM-for-hire') and is the author of all campaign-related emails and notes, establishing her as the GM.

No artifact provides a campaign creation date or any date that could be interpreted as the founding/start date of the campaign. Dates in the artifacts refer to real-world events or in-game events, not campaign creation.

While the campaign's tone and themes (low magic, intrigue, dark fantasy, etc.) are discussed, the explicit genre label 'Low Fantasy / Political Intrigue / Dark Mystery' is not stated or paraphrased together in any artifact.
There are references to historical/medieval settings, but the explicit TL3 (Late Medieval / Early Renaissance) designation is not present or paraphrased with sufficient clarity.
No artifact discusses TL exceptions or special technology levels in the campaign world.
There is mention of point budgets in house rules, but not the specific 125-point starting PCs and NPC range as described in the required field.
The house rules artifact refers to the campaign as 'Cinematic but Grounded' and discusses cinematic options, which matches the 'Cinematic' realism level in the required field.
While many campaign events and themes are referenced, there is no comprehensive synopsis or summary that matches the required field's detail and structure.
No artifact provides a timeline or list of historical events for the campaign world.
No artifact describes the value of the 'silver crown' or provides an in-world currency value equivalent.
No artifact describes the value of the 'copper penny' or its equivalence to other coins or real-world money.
No artifact describes the value of the 'gold lion' or its equivalence to other coins or real-world money.
No artifact discusses the use of precious metals in the campaign's currency.
No artifact discusses the presence or absence of paper money in the campaign.
No artifact discusses electronic money or its absence in the campaign.

'Lionsgate' is mentioned in the text artifact as the capital of Caldria.

'The Veiled Expanse' is explicitly mentioned in the email to Dr. Hart and discussed as a campaign plane.
The email to Dr. Hart describes the Veiled Expanse as a 'plane' and discusses its liminal, transit-like qualities, matching the 'Astral / Limbo-like Transit Plane' type.
The email to Dr. Hart provides a detailed, in-character description of the Veiled Expanse, including its appearance, time flow, and narrative function.
'The Ember Deep' is explicitly mentioned in the email to Dr. Hart and discussed as a campaign plane.
The email to Dr. Hart describes the Ember Deep as an 'elemental' and 'chthonic fire' plane, matching the required type.
The email to Dr. Hart provides a detailed, in-character description of the Ember Deep, including its geography, hazards, and narrative inspiration.
